title: Pollo en Mole Chicken Mole Sauce

seo.title: Authentic Chicken Mole Sauce Recipe | Pollo en Mole
seo.metaDescription: Learn how to make authentic Mexican Chicken Mole Sauce (Pollo en Mole) with our easy recipe. Rich, complex, and flavorful – perfect for your next meal!

seo.imageCaption: Savor the rich, complex flavors of homemade Mexican Chicken Mole Sauce, a true culinary delight.

description: Pollo en Mole is a celebrated Mexican dish where tender chicken is smothered in a rich, complex, and flavorful mole sauce. This recipe guides you through creating a balanced mole with a hint of sweetness and spice. It is a traditional delight perfect for any special occasion or a comforting family meal.

ingredients.notes: shredded or whole, such as thighs or breasts

instructions.title: Prepare the Chiles
instructions.text: Heat a dry skillet over medium heat. Toast the dried ancho, pasilla, and mulato chiles for 1-2 minutes per side until fragrant. Be careful not to burn them. Place the toasted chiles in a bowl and cover with hot water. Let them soak for 15-20 minutes until softened.

instructions.title: Toast Aromatics and Spices
instructions.text: In the same dry skillet, toast the almonds, sesame seeds, and corn tortilla pieces until lightly golden and fragrant. Remove and set aside. Add a small amount of oil to the skillet, then lightly char the halved tomatoes, chopped onion, and garlic cloves. Cook until softened and slightly browned.

instructions.title: Blend the Mole Paste
instructions.text: Drain the softened chiles and transfer them to a blender. Add the toasted almonds, sesame seeds, tortilla pieces, raisins, charred tomatoes, onion, and garlic. Pour in 1 cup of chicken broth. Blend until a smooth paste forms, adding more broth if needed to achieve a thick, pourable consistency.

instructions.title: Cook the Mole Sauce
instructions.text: Heat 2 tablespoons of vegetable oil in a large pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour in the blended mole paste. Stir constantly for about 10 minutes until the paste darkens and thickens. This step is crucial for developing flavor.

instructions.title: Finish the Sauce
instructions.text: Add the remaining chicken broth, Mexican chocolate, ground cinnamon, cloves, cumin, dried oregano, and salt to the pot. Stir well until the chocolate is fully melted and incorporated. Bring the sauce to a gentle simmer, then reduce heat to low and let it cook for at least 15 minutes, stirring occasionally, allowing the flavors to meld. The sauce should be rich and slightly thick.

instructions.title: Combine with Chicken
instructions.text: Add the cooked chicken pieces to the mole sauce. Stir gently to ensure all chicken is thoroughly coated. Continue to simmer for another 5-10 minutes, allowing the chicken to warm through and absorb the delicious mole flavors. Serve hot, garnished with extra sesame seeds if desired.

tips: For a deeper flavor, lightly toast whole spices like cloves and cumin seeds before grinding them.
Adjust the spice level by adding a small piece of habanero or serrano chile when blending the sauce, or reduce the amount of mulato chile for less heat.
Mole sauce can be made ahead of time and stored in the refrigerator for up to 3 days, or frozen for up to 3 months. It often tastes even better the next day.
article:
Embark on a culinary journey to the heart of Mexico with Pollo en Mole, a dish that transcends mere sustenance to become a celebration of heritage, flavor, and tradition. This iconic Mexican chicken recipe, featuring a rich, complex mole sauce, is a testament to the country's diverse gastronomic landscape. While the preparation can seem daunting, the reward of a homemade Chicken Mole Sauce is an unparalleled experience of deep, earthy flavors, subtle heat, and a hint of sweetness that dances on the palate.

## What is Chicken Mole Sauce?

At its core, mole (pronounced MOH-lay) is a generic term for a variety of Mexican sauces, often characterized by their intricate blend of ingredients. Of these, Mole Poblano is arguably the most famous and the one most commonly associated with Pollo en Mole, or chicken in mole sauce. This legendary sauce hails from the state of Puebla and is a masterpiece of culinary alchemy. It typically features a base of several types of dried chiles, nuts, seeds, spices, fruits, and a touch of chocolate, all meticulously ground and simmered to create a velvety, multidimensional sauce.

The flavor profile of an authentic Chicken Mole Sauce is incredibly complex: it’s smoky from toasted chiles, nutty from almonds and peanuts, aromatic from cinnamon and cloves, subtly sweet from raisins and a hint of chocolate, and savory from garlic and onion. The heat level can vary from mild to moderately spicy, depending on the chiles used and their preparation. More than just a sauce, mole is a cultural symbol, often served at significant celebrations and family gatherings, representing a long-standing tradition of culinary artistry.

## Ingredients for Authentic Chicken Mole Sauce

Crafting an authentic Chicken Mole Sauce requires a thoughtful selection of ingredients. While the list may seem extensive, each component plays a crucial role in building the sauce's characteristic depth and complexity.

**For the Mole Sauce:**
*   **Dried Chiles:** 4-5 Ancho chiles (for fruity, smoky flavor), 4-5 Guajillo chiles (for mild fruitiness), 2-3 Pasilla chiles (for earthy, smoky notes).
*   **Aromatics:** 1 medium white onion (roughly chopped), 4-5 cloves garlic.
*   **Spices:** 1/2 tsp cumin seeds, 1/2 tsp coriander seeds, 1/4 tsp whole black peppercorns, 2-3 whole cloves, 1/2-inch piece Mexican cinnamon stick (or 1/4 tsp ground cinnamon), a tiny pinch of star anise (optional).
*   **Nuts & Seeds:** 1/4 cup blanched almonds, 1/4 cup peanuts, 2 tbsp sesame seeds, 1 tbsp pumpkin seeds (pepitas).
*   **Thickeners & Flavor Enhancers:** 2 slices stale bread (bolillo or similar), 2 corn tortillas (torn), 1 ripe tomato (roughly chopped), 1/4 cup raisins or prunes.
*   **Fat:** 2-3 tbsp lard or vegetable oil.
*   **Liquid:** 4-6 cups chicken broth (preferably homemade).
*   **Chocolate:** 1 oz Mexican chocolate (dark, often with cinnamon, like Ibarra or Taza).
*   **Seasoning:** Salt to taste, a pinch of sugar (optional, to balance bitterness).

**For the Chicken:**
*   3-4 lbs whole chicken, cut into pieces (or just bone-in, skin-on thighs and drumsticks).
*   1/2 white onion, 2-3 cloves garlic, 1 bay leaf, salt (for simmering chicken).

## Step-by-Step Instructions for Pollo en Mole

Making Pollo en Mole is a labor of love, but each step builds towards an incredibly rewarding dish.

1.  **Prepare the Chicken:** In a large pot, combine chicken pieces, 1/2 onion, garlic cloves, bay leaf, and a generous pinch of salt. Cover with water and bring to a boil. Reduce heat, skim any foam, and simmer for 30-40 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and tender. Remove chicken and set aside. Strain and reserve the broth (you'll need about 4-6 cups for the mole).
2.  **Prepare the Chiles:** Carefully remove stems and seeds from the dried chiles. Heat a dry comal or skillet over medium-low heat. Toast the chiles for 1-2 minutes per side until fragrant but not burnt. Be careful, as burnt chiles will make the sauce bitter. Transfer toasted chiles to a bowl, cover with hot water or reserved chicken broth, and let them rehydrate for 20-30 minutes until soft.
3.  **Toast Nuts, Seeds & Spices:** In the same dry skillet, toast almonds, peanuts, sesame seeds, and pumpkin seeds separately until lightly golden and fragrant. Set aside. Lightly toast the cumin seeds, coriander seeds, peppercorns, cloves, and cinnamon stick until aromatic. Set aside with the nuts and seeds.
4.  **Sauté Aromatics & Other Ingredients:** Heat 1-2 tbsp lard or oil in a large pot or Dutch oven. Sauté the chopped onion and garlic until softened and translucent, about 5-7 minutes. Add the chopped tomato, raisins/prunes, bread, and torn tortillas. Cook for another 5-7 minutes, stirring occasionally, until everything is slightly softened and caramelized.
5.  **Blend the Sauce:** Drain the rehydrated chiles (reserving a bit of the soaking liquid). Combine the rehydrated chiles, sautéed aromatics, toasted nuts, seeds, and spices in a high-speed blender. Add 2-3 cups of the reserved chicken broth. Blend until very smooth. This may require blending in batches. For an exceptionally smooth Chicken Mole Sauce, strain the mixture through a fine-mesh sieve, pressing firmly to extract all the liquid and discarding any solids.
6.  **Cook the Mole:** Heat the remaining 1-2 tbsp lard or oil in your large pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour in the blended mole sauce. It will splatter, so be cautious. Bring the sauce to a simmer, then reduce heat to low. This is the most crucial step for flavor development. Simmer the mole, stirring frequently to prevent sticking, for at least 45 minutes to 1.5 hours. The sauce will darken, thicken, and its flavors will deepen significantly. Add more chicken broth as needed to maintain a pourable but thick consistency.
7.  **Add Chocolate and Season:** Stir in the Mexican chocolate until it completely melts and is incorporated into the sauce. Season with salt to taste, and add a pinch of sugar if the sauce seems too bitter or needs a touch of balance.
8.  **Combine Chicken and Mole:** Gently add the cooked chicken pieces to the simmering mole sauce. Allow them to simmer together for at least 15-20 minutes, or longer, so the chicken can absorb the rich flavors of the Chicken Mole Sauce.

## Pro Tips for the Best Chicken Mole Sauce

*   **Quality Chiles are Key:** Seek out fresh, pliable dried chiles. Avoid those that are brittle or dusty.
*   **Don't Burn the Chiles:** Toasting chiles brings out their flavor, but burning them will impart an unpleasant bitterness to your sauce. Watch them carefully!
*   **Blend Until Silky Smooth:** A powerful blender is your friend here. For the most authentic, restaurant-quality mole, straining the blended sauce through a fine-mesh sieve is highly recommended.
*   **Patience in Simmering:** The long, slow simmer is where the magic happens. It allows all the complex flavors to meld and deepen, transforming the individual ingredients into a harmonious masterpiece. Don't rush this step.
*   **Taste and Adjust:** Mole is all about balance. Taste frequently and adjust seasoning. You might need more salt, a touch of sugar to cut bitterness, or a little more broth for consistency.
*   **Make Ahead:** Mole often tastes even better the next day, allowing the flavors to fully develop and settle. It also freezes beautifully.

## Serving Suggestions for Chicken Mole

Pollo en Mole is traditionally served simply, allowing the complex sauce to shine.
*   **Classic Pairing:** Serve generously over warm, fluffy white rice (Arroz Blanco) or Mexican red rice (Arroz Rojo), accompanied by a stack of warm corn tortillas.
*   **Garnishes:** A sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds, finely chopped white onion, and fresh cilantro adds visual appeal and a burst of freshness. A dollop of Mexican crema or sour cream can also offer a cooling contrast.
*   **Sides:** Refried beans or a simple green salad can round out the meal beautifully.
*   **Leftovers:** Leftover Chicken Mole Sauce is fantastic for making enchiladas, tamales, or even quesadillas.

## Variations and Substitutions

While Mole Poblano is a classic, the world of mole is vast.
*   **Different Moles:** Explore other types like Mole Verde (green mole, typically with tomatillos and herbs), Mole Negro (even darker, often with more chiles and chocolate), or Mole Amarillo (yellow mole).
*   **Protein Alternatives:** While chicken is traditional, mole pairs wonderfully with turkey, pork, or even duck. For a vegetarian option, try it with roasted vegetables, mushrooms, or firm tofu.
*   **Chile Adjustments:** Adjust the number and type of chiles to control the heat and flavor. For more heat, add a few chiles de árbol or serrano peppers.
*   **Nut/Seed Changes:** If you have allergies or preferences, you can adjust the type of nuts and seeds. Walnuts, pecans, or even pepitas can be used.
*   **Chocolate Choices:** If Mexican chocolate isn't available, a good quality dark chocolate (70-80% cacao) with a pinch of cinnamon can be substituted.

Making Pollo en Mole is more than just cooking; it's an immersive culinary experience that connects you to generations of Mexican tradition. The effort is undoubtedly worth it for the rich, unforgettable flavors of this magnificent Chicken Mole Sauce. Give it a try, and savor the magic of Mexico in your own kitchen.
